preamble.summary: A royal liturgy spoken by the congregation on behalf of the king as he prepares to go to war. The people pray for God to answer the king from his holy place, to send help from the sanctuary and support from Zion, to remember his offerings and accept his sacrifices. The psalm builds to a declaration of trust: 'Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we call on the name of the LORD our God.' It ends with a final petition for the king's victory and God's answer.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 20 is a rare window into the liturgical life of the pre-exilic temple. The congregation speaks to and about the king, praying for his success before battle. The structure suggests a ceremony: the people speak their blessings (vv. 2-6), a solo voice (perhaps a priest or prophet) declares confidence in divine anointing (v. 7), the people affirm their trust (vv. 8-9), and all join in the final petition (v. 10). The theological center is verse 8: the distinction between trusting in military technology (chariots and horses) and trusting in the name (shem) of the LORD. The 'name' in Israelite theology is not a label but the accessible presence of God — to invoke the name is to invoke the person. The psalm insists that Israel's military strategy is ultimately theological: they go to war in the name of the LORD, not in the strength of their arsenal.
preamble.friction: The psalm assumes the legitimacy of the Davidic monarchy and the efficacy of temple sacrifice — the king's offerings and burnt sacrifices (vv. 4) are presented as genuine means of securing divine favor. For post-exilic readers (and for all later readers without king or temple), the psalm requires transposition: from literal king to awaited messiah, from physical temple to spiritual worship, from military battle to spiritual conflict. The psalm also raises the question of how divine favor relates to military outcome — Israel did not always win its wars, even when the king sacrificed and the people prayed.
preamble.connections: Psalm 21 is the companion piece — where Psalm 20 prays for victory before battle, Psalm 21 gives thanks for victory after battle. The pair forms a liturgical unit. The chariots-and-horses statement (v. 8) echoes Deuteronomy 17:16, which prohibits the king from multiplying horses (military power), and Isaiah 31:1, which warns against relying on Egyptian cavalry. The phrase 'the name of the LORD our God' (shem YHVH Eloheinu) connects to the third commandment's prohibition against carrying God's name for emptiness (Exodus 20:7) — here the name is carried into battle as a banner of trust.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Verse 1 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: יַֽעַנְךָ֣ יְ֭הוָה בְּי֣וֹם צָרָ֑ה יְ֝שַׂגֶּבְךָ֗ שֵׁ֤ם ׀ אֱלֹהֵ֬י יַעֲקֹֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD hear thee in the day of trouble; the name of the God of Jacob defend thee;
verses.rendering:
May the LORD answer you in the day of trouble;
may the name of the God of Jacob set you safely on high.
verses.expanded_rendering: The congregation addresses the king directly: ya'ankha YHVH be-yom tsarah ('may the LORD answer you on the day of distress'). The 'day of trouble' (yom tsarah) is the specific moment of military crisis — the eve of battle, the hour when the outcome hangs in the balance. The second line: yesaggevkha shem Elohei Ya'aqov ('may the name of the God of Jacob lift you to a secure height'). The verb sagav means 'to be high, to be inaccessibly elevated' — like a fortress on a cliff that cannot be reached by attackers. The agent of this protection is not God in the abstract but shem Elohei Ya'aqov — 'the name of the God of Jacob.' The shem ('name') is the accessible presence of God, the part of God that can be invoked, called upon, and carried. The specific title 'God of Jacob' recalls the patriarch who wrestled with God and prevailed — the God who fights for and with his chosen.
translator_notes: The 'name' (shem) of God in Israelite theology functions as a near-synonym for God's presence. The temple was the place where God caused his name to dwell (Deuteronomy 12:5). To pray in God's name is to access God himself through the revealed identity he has shared.

key_terms.semantic_range: name, reputation, renown, identity, the accessible presence of a person or God
key_terms.note: In Israelite thought, a name is not merely a label but contains the essence and authority of the person it represents. God's shem is his self-revelation — the identity he has made known, through which human beings can access his presence and power.

verses.text_kjv: Send thee help from the sanctuary, and strengthen thee out of Zion;
verses.rendering:
May he send you help from the sanctuary
and support you from Zion.
translator_notes: The help (ezer) comes from qodesh ('the holy place, the sanctuary') and from Zion — the physical location where God's presence dwells. The theology is spatial: divine aid emanates from the temple and travels to the battlefield. The sanctuary is not merely a place of worship but a base of operations for divine intervention.

verses.text_kjv: Remember all thy offerings, and accept thy burnt sacrifice; Selah.
verses.rendering:
May he remember all your grain offerings
and accept your burnt offering. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The prayer now addresses the king's pre-battle sacrifices. yizkhor kol minchotekha ('may he remember all your grain offerings') — the verb zakhar ('to remember') in relation to God means more than mental recall; it means to act on the basis of what is remembered. When God 'remembers' an offering, he responds to it with favor. ve-olatekha yedasheneh ('and your burnt offering may he regard as fat/acceptable') — the verb dashen means 'to make fat, to regard as rich,' and in sacrificial contexts it means to accept the offering as satisfying, as sufficient. The selah that follows marks a liturgical pause — perhaps the moment when the sacrifice was actually offered and the congregation waited for a sign of acceptance.
translator_notes: The minchah ('grain offering') and olah ('burnt offering') represent the king's pre-battle worship. The king does not march to war without first offering sacrifice — the military campaign is embedded in liturgical practice. The army's strength begins at the altar.

verses.text_kjv: Grant thee according to thine own heart, and fulfil all thy counsel.
verses.rendering:
May he grant you your heart's desire
and fulfill all your plans.
translator_notes: The congregation prays that God will align the outcome with the king's intention: kilvavekha ('according to your heart') and kol atsatekha ('all your counsel/strategy'). The assumption is that the king's plans are righteous — his heart's desire is for the good of the nation and the glory of God. The prayer asks God to give the king what he seeks.

verses.text_hebrew: נְרַנְּנָ֤ה ׀ בִּ֘ישׁ֤וּעָתֶ֗ךָ וּבְשֵׁם־אֱלֹהֵ֥ינוּ נִדְגֹּ֑ל יְמַלֵּ֥א יְ֝הוָ֗ה כׇּל־מִשְׁאֲלוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: We will rejoice in thy salvation, and in the name of our God we will set up our banners: the LORD fulfil all thy petitions.
verses.rendering:
We will shout for joy over your victory
and in the name of our God raise our banners!
May the LORD fulfill all your requests.
verses.expanded_rendering: The congregation now looks forward to the celebration after victory: nerannenah bishu'atekha ('we will shout in triumph at your salvation/victory'). The verb ranan describes a loud, high-pitched cry of joy — a shout of triumph that fills the air. u-ve-shem Eloheinu nidgol ('and in the name of our God we will raise our banners') — the verb dagal means 'to raise a banner, to fly a flag, to display a standard.' The army's banner bears not the king's insignia but God's name. This is the visual counterpart to verse 8's theological statement: Israel goes to war under God's banner, not under the banner of military technology. The final clause returns to petition: yemalle YHVH kol mish'alotekha ('may the LORD fulfill all your petitions'). The word mish'alot ('requests, petitions') covers everything the king has asked God for in his pre-battle prayers.
translator_notes: The banner (degel) was a tribal and military standard in ancient Israel (Numbers 2:2). Here the congregation declares that their banner displays God's name rather than a tribal emblem or royal crest. The battle belongs to the LORD, and the banner makes that claim visible.

verses.text_hebrew: עַתָּ֤ה יָדַ֗עְתִּי כִּ֤י הוֹשִׁ֥יעַ ׀ יְהוָ֗ה מְשִׁ֫יח֥וֹ יַ֭עֲנֵהוּ מִשְּׁמֵ֣י קׇדְשׁ֑וֹ בִּ֝גְבֻר֗וֹת יֵ֣שַׁע יְמִינֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Now know I that the LORD saveth his anointed; he will hear him from his holy heaven with the saving strength of his right hand.
verses.expanded_rendering: A single voice — perhaps a priest, prophet, or the worship leader — breaks from the congregation with a declaration of certainty: attah yadati ('now I know!'). The verb yada ('to know') is not speculation but conviction. ki hoshia YHVH meshicho ('that the LORD saves his anointed') — the mashiach ('anointed one') is the king, consecrated by oil for his royal office. God saves the one he anointed — the anointing and the salvation belong together. ya'anehu mi-shemei qodesho ('he answers him from his holy heavens') — the answer comes from heaven itself, not merely from the earthly sanctuary. bi-gevurot yesha yemino ('with the mighty acts of salvation of his right hand') — gevurot ('mighty deeds, powers') and yesha ('salvation, victory') combine with yamin ('right hand') to describe God's most potent intervention. The right hand of God is the hand that splits seas, defeats armies, and establishes kings.
translator_notes: The shift to first-person singular ('now I know') may indicate a prophetic oracle within the liturgy — a moment when a single voice announces divine assurance to the congregation and the king. This voice transforms the congregation's prayer into confident knowledge.

key_terms.semantic_range: his anointed one, the one he has consecrated with oil for a sacred role
key_terms.note: meshicho ('his anointed') is the possessive form — this is not any anointed figure but God's own anointed, the king whom God personally chose and set apart. The anointing creates a bond: what threatens the anointed threatens God's own investment.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֤לֶּה בָרֶ֣כֶב וְאֵ֣לֶּה בַסּוּסִ֑ים וַאֲנַ֓חְנוּ ׀ בְּשֵׁם־יְהוָ֖ה אֱלֹהֵ֣ינוּ נַזְכִּֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: Some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God.
verses.rendering:
Some trust in chariots and some in horses,
but we call on the name of the LORD our God.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the psalm's theological thesis. elleh va-rekhev ve-elleh va-susim ('these in chariots and these in horses') — the demonstrative elleh ('these') points at other nations, other armies, other kings who base their confidence on military hardware. Chariots were the supreme weapon of the ancient battlefield — the tanks of the Bronze and Iron Ages. Horses provided cavalry speed and shock force. The psalm names the best technology available and then dismisses it: va-anachnu be-shem YHVH Eloheinu nazkir ('but we — in the name of the LORD our God we invoke, we proclaim, we call to remembrance'). The verb zakar (Hiphil, 'to cause to remember, to invoke, to mention') means to speak the name of God as an act of trust — to make God's name the basis of confidence rather than the number of chariots in the stable. This is not pacifism (David fought wars) but a declaration about the ultimate source of security: not what you possess but whom you trust.
translator_notes: The distinction between trusting in chariots/horses and trusting in God's name is foundational to Israelite theology. Deuteronomy 17:16 prohibits the king from multiplying horses. Isaiah 31:1 pronounces woe on those who go to Egypt for horses. The psalm institutionalizes this warning as congregational confession: our security is the name, not the arsenal.

key_terms.note: The chariot was the dominant offensive weapon of the ancient Near East from the late Bronze Age onward. Egypt, Assyria, and the Canaanite city-states all relied on chariot forces. Israel's theological tradition consistently warns against trusting in this technology, insisting that divine power supersedes military power.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֭מָּה כָּרְע֣וּ וְנָפָ֑לוּ וַאֲנַ֥חְנוּ קַּ֝֗מְנוּ וַנִּתְעוֹדָֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: They are brought down and fallen: but we are risen, and stand upright.
verses.rendering:
They collapse and fall,
but we rise and stand firm.
verses.expanded_rendering: The result of the two different trusts is stated with stark brevity. hemmah kare'u ve-nafalu ('they — they have bowed down and fallen') — the verb kara ('to bow, kneel, collapse') describes the buckling of the knees, the loss of strength. nafal ('to fall') completes the descent: they are on the ground. va-anachnu qamnu vannitodad ('but we — we have risen and stood upright'). The verb qum ('to rise, stand') is the opposite of kara ('to collapse'). The verb hitodad ('to stand firm, to rally, to brace oneself') is rare and powerful — it means to collect oneself, to gather strength, to stand steady when everything else is falling. The contrast is total: those who trusted in chariots are on their knees; those who invoked the name are on their feet.
translator_notes: The perfect tenses ('they have collapsed,' 'we have risen') may be prophetic perfects — describing the future outcome with such certainty that past tense is used. The congregation speaks of the battle's result as already accomplished, expressing faith that God's answer is certain.

verses.text_kjv: Save, LORD: let the king hear us when we call.
verses.rendering:
LORD, save the king!
Answer us on the day we call.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends with a final petition that brings everything together. YHVH hoshi'ah ('LORD, save!') — the most basic cry of the Psalter, the imperative that underlies all prayer. hammelekh ya'anenu ve-yom qor'enu ('may the king answer us on the day we call') — this line is textually ambiguous. It can be read as 'save, LORD! May the king answer us when we call' (addressing both God and the king) or as 'LORD, save the king! Answer us on the day we call' (addressing God alone on behalf of the king and the people). The second reading is more likely: the congregation asks God to save the king and to answer the people. The psalm that began with prayer for the king ends with prayer for the people themselves — the king's salvation and the people's salvation are inseparable. When God answers the king, he answers the nation.
translator_notes: The textual division of this verse is debated. The Masoretic accents suggest 'LORD, save! May the king answer us on the day we call.' But many translators read 'LORD, save the king! Answer us on the day we call.' The ambiguity may be deliberate, allowing the verse to function as both a prayer for the king and a prayer for the congregation.
